title: Fairy cakes with glacé icing
subtitle: Classic British bun-tin cakes with a simple flat icing
excerpt: Small vanilla sponge cakes baked in a bun tin and finished with a poured glacé icing. Lighter and smaller than a cupcake, with a flat rather than piped top.
sourceNotes: Fairy cakes are a distinctly British form of small individual cake, distinct from the American cupcake in that they are smaller, lighter, and finished with poured rather than piped icing. They appear in British baking guides from the early twentieth century and are documented in Delia Smith's Complete Cookery Course (1978), Marguerite Patten's Cookery in Colour (1960), and Mary Berry's Baking Bible (1994). The name 'fairy' reflects their lighter, more delicate character relative to a heavier tea cake.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze un-iced cakes in a bag for up to 2 months. Ice after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The un-iced cakes keep well for 2 days. Glace ice on the day of serving.

baking.decoratingTechnique: glacé icing poured and left to set flat

glossaryTerms.definition: A simple icing made from icing sugar and a liquid (water, lemon juice, or milk) mixed to a pourable consistency. It sets to a smooth, slightly shiny flat surface as it dries. Thinner than buttercream and applied by spooning rather than piping.

content.text: Fairy cakes are smaller than muffin-tin cupcakes: a bun tin holds about 40 ml per hole, so the cakes are roughly half the size. That scale matters. A heaped teaspoon of batter per hole will give a neat dome that rises just above the rim; any more and the top spreads flat and may overflow.

The glacé icing needs to be worked fast. Once it begins to set (within 2 to 3 minutes of mixing), it becomes difficult to spread and starts to drag. Mix in small batches and ice 6 cakes at a time while the icing is still completely fluid.

attrs.prepNote: freshly squeezed, or use water for a plain icing

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line two 12-hole bun tins with paper cases. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time, then the {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}}. Place a heaped teaspoon of batter into each case. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until risen, golden, and springy to the touch. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack to cool completely.

, sift the {{icing-sugar}} into a bowl and stir in the {{lemon-juice}} a tablespoon at a time until the icing falls from a spoon in a thick ribbon. Spoon a teaspoon onto each cake and spread lightly to the edges. Leave undisturbed for 20 minutes to set completely before storing.
